title: Applying a colour-wash glaze to plaster walls
subtitle: Dilute emulsion paint with glaze medium and apply in irregular strokes for a translucent, layered decorative effect
excerpt: How to apply a colour-wash glaze to painted or plaster walls. Covers choosing a compatible base coat, mixing the glaze, and applying the wash in stages to build a translucent, layered effect without drips or hard edges.
sourceNotes: Decorative paint finishing techniques in Collins Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). UK decorating trade practice for glaze-work finishes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A transparent or semi-transparent slow-drying acrylic or oil-based medium added to paint to extend working time and increase translucency. Mixed with emulsion paint in ratios of 3:1 to 6:1 (glaze to paint) for colour-wash work. Acrylic glaze is water-based and cleans up easily; oil glaze requires white spirit for cleanup but stays open longer for elaborate faux-finish work. Available in DIY stores under names such as scumble glaze, acrylic glaze, or faux-finish medium.

content.text: A colour-wash glaze is a diluted, translucent paint layer brushed over a solid base coat in loose strokes. It gives walls a depth and movement that flat emulsion does not (the base shows through the glaze irregularly, giving a hand-applied, textural result. The approach is mixing emulsion with
, applying in two stages, and blending the edges before the surface dries.

The base coat should be a flat or eggshell emulsion) a mid-tone or light colour one shade lighter or significantly lighter than the glaze colour. Apply two even coats of base and allow to dry fully before glazing. A smooth surface gives the best translucency result; textured surfaces produce a more broken, rustic effect because the glaze pools in the hollows.

Mix 1 part emulsion paint to 4 parts acrylic glaze medium. Stir thoroughly. Test the consistency on a spare board: the mixed glaze should be fluid enough to spread in thin translucent strokes but hold a visible brush mark when dry. Too thin and the effect disappears; too thick and the glaze drags.

Load the 50 mm brush lightly (less than for normal painting. Work in an irregular cross-hatch or X-stroke, varying the angle and pressure so no two brush marks are parallel. Work in sections no larger than 600 × 600 mm. As each section is applied, blend the edges immediately with a dry brush dragged across the wet surface in loose strokes. Keep a wet edge to avoid lap marks at the section joins. Allow to dry fully) typically 2 hours.

A second, lighter wash layer adds depth and hides any hard-edged patches from the first. Apply more sparingly (load the brush even less) and use the same cross-hatch stroke. The second layer can be a slightly different tone for a richer result. Blend at the edges. Allow to dry before assessing the final effect.

attrs.symptom: Visible lap marks where sections meet
attrs.cause: The edge of the previous section dried before the next section was applied, so the overlapping wet glaze builds up a darker line.
attrs.fix: Work with a second person: one applies the glaze, one blends immediately. Alternatively, work smaller sections and keep a wetter edge by loading the brush more at the section boundary.
